title: Penang packaging / OSAT cluster
what_happened: A dense back-end semiconductor cluster has developed over decades around Penang, spanning packaging, assembly, test, equipment and materials, and semiconductor engineering.
relevance: Illustrates a mature regional back-end ecosystem: 350+ foreign firms and 4,000 SMEs in Penang, with ~13% of global back-end capacity; representative firms include Intel, Infineon, AMD, Micron, ASE and AT&S.
limitation: Cluster-level scale does not describe any specific partner's economics, capacity, or customer overlap; UMC-specific figures are not established here.
discussion_prompt: Malaysia's packaging/OSAT ecosystem may be relevant to future partnership discussions.

title: Intel Malaysia advanced packaging expansion
what_happened: Intel expanded advanced packaging and assembly/test operations in Malaysia, adding to its long-standing footprint.
relevance: Shows Malaysia's ability to host sophisticated back-end and advanced-packaging operations at multinational scale.
limitation: Intel's internal packaging economics do not indicate third-party partnership economics; specific investment amount and capacity should be confirmed against primary sources before use.

title: Amkor Technology Vietnam campus
what_happened: Amkor established a large advanced system-in-package assembly and test manufacturing campus in northern Vietnam.
where: Yen Phong II-C Industrial Park, Bac Ninh, Vietnam
relevance: Indicates Vietnam's growing back-end and electronics manufacturing gravity alongside heavy FDI (Samsung, Intel).
limitation: A single foreign-invested campus does not describe local supply-chain depth (front-end nearly blank) or broader ecosystem maturity; capacity/workforce detail needs source validation.
discussion_prompt: Vietnam's EMS and electronics base may be relevant to customer-support discussions.

title: Thailand EEC electronics / automotive cluster & Infineon packaging
what_happened: Thailand's Eastern Economic Corridor concentrates automotive, electronics and PCB/HDD manufacturing; Infineon is building an advanced-packaging fab, and new clusters are forming at Lamphun/Lampang.
value_chain_layer: EMS / electronics demand; back-end / advanced packaging
relevance: Thailand is ASEAN's largest PCB producer with a deep automotive and power-electronics base and back-end presence.
limitation: Cluster and demand signals do not describe named wafer demand or specialty-process economics; front-end and advanced-packaging build-out is early.
discussion_prompt: Thailand's automotive and power-electronics base may be relevant to mature-node demand monitoring.

title: Philippines ATP base (TI and the wider ecosystem)
what_happened: The Philippines hosts a long-standing assembly, test and packaging (ATP) base; Texas Instruments and others run semiconductor assembly/test operations, and the OECD has profiled the domestic ecosystem.
where: Baguio, Clark, CALABARZON, Metro Manila and Cebu, Philippines
relevance: The Philippines is the world's 9th largest chip exporter (73% SMS / 27% EMS) with an English-speaking workforce.
limitation: The base is ATP-heavy with very little IC design (~13 firms, ~1,000 designers); high electricity price and reliability are constraints; support-workload specifics are not established here.
discussion_prompt: The Philippines' ATP base may be relevant to back-end or support discussions.

title: Indonesia Batam packaging & Infineon ATP
what_happened: Indonesia's semiconductor activity is concentrated at Batam (adjacent to Singapore), where Infineon runs ATP operations; a silica-downstreaming roadmap aims to build domestic wafer capability.
where: Batam, Riau Islands; Java industrial zones, Indonesia
relevance: Batam's proximity to Singapore and EV/local-content demand are the entry drivers; the Silica Downstreaming Roadmap 2025–2035 signals policy intent.
limitation: Ecosystem and supply chain are the weakest of the five countries; electronic-grade processing infrastructure remains a key challenge.

title: ARM × Danantara Indonesia talent-training agreement
what_happened: On 23 Feb 2026, Indonesia's sovereign fund Danantara and UK-based ARM signed a framework agreement in London (witnessed by President Prabowo and ARM CEO Rene Haas) to train 15,000 Indonesian engineers within ARM's ecosystem. Indonesia is investing ~US$150M initially to build a national semiconductor ecosystem; the first cohort of ~1,000 started in May 2026, and cooperation extends to next-generation chip design across six IP domains (automotive, IoT, data center, home appliances, autonomous, quantum), with IP held by Indonesia.
relevance: A concrete, dated policy signal on design-talent development and which architecture (ARM) shapes that talent. Facts (amount, headcount, timeline, IP domains) are source-backed.
limitation: The 'ecosystem lock-in' and 'RISC-V defense' readings are analyst inference, not stated in the agreement. Indonesia's expectation that this leads to chip-manufacturing investment is its own; ARM's commitment is design talent and architecture, not fabrication — a gap between design-side lock-in and manufacturing landing remains open.
discussion_prompt: Indonesia's ARM / Danantara case may be relevant as a design-talent and policy signal; note that talent competition is not only about numbers but about whose architecture shapes the talent.
